app.title: Math with Smiles
app.subtitle: Learn with the Ten Frames method

game.countingDesc: Use Ten Frames to learn counting from 0 to 10!
game.additionDesc: There are already {{num1}} dots. Add {{num2}} more!
game.additionHint: Tap empty cells to add dots!
game.subtractionDesc: There are {{num1}} dots. Remove {{num2}} of them!
game.subtractionHint: Tap on dots to remove them!
game.puzzleDesc: There are {{number}} dots. How many more to make 10?

feedback.keepTrying: Keep going, you can do it!

info.title: About Ten Frames
info.whatDesc: A Ten Frame is a simple yet powerful visual tool: a rectangular frame divided into two rows of five cells, making ten cells in total. Developed as a teaching aid in the 1980s and rooted in mathematics education research, Ten Frames are now a cornerstone of early numeracy programs worldwide — including Common Core (US), the Australian and Canadian Curricula, UK Early Years Foundation Stage, New Zealand's Numeracy Project, and Singapore Math.
info.howHelp: How Do They Help Children Learn?
info.howHelpDesc: Ten Frames develop subitizing — the ability to instantly recognize a quantity without counting one by one. Children learn to see groups: 3 on top and 2 on the bottom means 5. A full row is always 5, a full frame is always 10. These benchmarks become anchors for all future mental math. For ages 5-7, Ten Frames provide a bridge from concrete to abstract thinking (the CPA approach used in Singapore Math).
info.countingDesc: In counting mode, children tap cells to fill them one at a time. Each filled cell represents one object, building one-to-one correspondence — the understanding that each number word matches exactly one object. Children can see at a glance whether they have "a few" or "a lot," developing early quantity estimation skills.
info.additionDesc: The first addend appears in one color and the second in another. Children fill cells for the second number and visually see both parts and the whole. For example, 3 + 4: three cells are placed, the child adds four more to see seven total. This also teaches the 'make ten' strategy: for 8 + 5, fill to 10 (add 2) and see 3 left over — discovering that 8 + 5 = 13.
info.subtractionDesc: The starting number fills the frame, and children tap to remove objects. For 7 − 3, seven cells are filled and the child removes three to see four remaining. This "take away" model makes subtraction tangible — children literally see the quantity shrinking.
info.bondsDesc: The puzzle mode teaches complements of 10 — if 6 cells are filled, how many empty cells remain? Children discover that 6 + 4 = 10, 7 + 3 = 10, and so on. Knowing these number bonds fluently is essential for mental math strategies.
info.benefitsDesc:
• Visual and concrete number understanding
• Instant quantity recognition (subitizing)
• Strong number sense foundation
• Fluent mental addition and subtraction
• Reduced math anxiety through playful exploration
• Natural benchmarks of 5 and 10 for calculation
• Bridge from concrete to abstract thinking (CPA approach)
info.tipsParentsDesc:
• Let the child explore freely — there's no wrong way to fill a ten frame!
• Ask questions like "How many more do you need to make 10?"
• Celebrate effort, not just correct answers
• Short daily sessions (5–10 minutes) are more effective than long ones
• Connect ten frames to real life: "We have 3 apples, how many more to fill our box of 10?"
info.researchDesc: Ten Frames are recommended by the National Council of Teachers of Mathematics (NCTM) as a key tool for developing number sense in early childhood. Research shows that children who use ten frames develop stronger mental math skills and a deeper understanding of place value. The method is part of the Common Core State Standards in the United States and is used in curricula worldwide, including Singapore Math and Australian National Curriculum.
